title: Merged Exploration
description: Each 32-bit pixel expands to ~4-5 bytes. We need strategies that extract more entropy from the 28 param bits. + What concrete encoding strategy improvements would help pixelpack achieve better than 7:1 bits-to-output ratio? Current bottleneck: each 32-bit pixel only expands to ~4-5 bytes average via dictionary lookup. Ideas must work within the existing seed format (4-bit strategy selector + 28-bit params). Think about: variable-length dictionary indices, back-references to previous pixel output, arithmetic coding of common byte patterns, context-dependent expansion where pixel N's output depends on pixel N-1's output.

children.title: Each 32-bit pixel expands to ~4-5 bytes. We need strategies that extract more en
children.description: Each 32-bit pixel expands to ~4-5 bytes. We need strategies that extract more entropy from the 28 param bits.

children.title: Context-Dependent Expansion
children.description: Pixel N can reference pixel N-1 output. A pixel with strategy CTX_COPY copies the last K bytes from the previous expansion and appends new bytes. This means repeated patterns (indentation, variable names) cost 0 bits after first occurrence.
enables: Back-references eliminate redundant bytes across pixels
Run-length patterns in code (indentation, repeated keywords) become nearly free
Enables fractal unfold: each pixels output feeds into next pixels context
children.risk: Breaks independent pixel decoding -- must decode sequentially. Makes PNG order matter.

children.title: LZ77-style back-ref strategy
children.description: Strategy bit=0xC means: params encode (offset, length) back-reference into the running output buffer. 14 bits offset + 14 bits length = copy up to 16KB back, 16KB length.
enables: Replaces repeated substrings with 32-bit pixels
Could encode 200-byte programs with many repeated tokens in ~10 pixels
children.risk: Need a running buffer during decode. More complex state.

children.title: Delta encoding between pixels
children.description: Pixel N stores XOR deltas from pixel N-1 output. If consecutive code segments share prefixes (like "    " indentation), the delta is tiny and the pixel can store mostly literal new content.
enables: Code with consistent indentation compresses very well
Natural for multi-line programs
children.risk: One corrupted pixel cascades. Need error detection.

children.title: Shared dictionary built during decode
children.description: As each pixel expands, common byte sequences get added to a dynamic dictionary (like LZ78/LZW). Later pixels can reference dictionary entries by short index.
enables: Dictionary adapts to the specific program being encoded
Common patterns like "def ", "return ", "print(" become single references after first use
children.risk: Dictionary state must be deterministic. Different programs build different dicts.

children.title: Chained Huffman per-pixel
children.description: Each pixel re-encodes its output using a Huffman table derived from the frequency distribution of all previous pixels output. Later pixels use shorter codes for bytes seen frequently so far.
enables: Adaptive compression that learns the programs own patterns
Could approach entropy limit for repetitive code
children.risk: Complex. Huffman table itself needs encoding. May not fit in 28 param bits.

children.title: Variable-Length Dictionary Indices
children.description: Current strategies use fixed-width indices (4-bit or 5-bit) into dictionaries. Waste bits on common entries. Use variable-length: top 8 entries get 3-bit indices, next 16 get 5-bit, etc. Fits more dictionary lookups into the same 28 param bits.
enables: More dictionary hits per pixel = more bytes per pixel
Common tokens (print, def, return, spaces) use fewer bits
Can pack 8-10 lookups instead of 5-7 into one pixel
children.risk: Parsing variable-length fields from a 28-bit word is tricky. Need unambiguous bit boundaries.

children.title: Huffman-coded dictionary index
children.description: Build a frequency table from corpus. Assign shorter bit codes to more common dictionary entries. 28 param bits become a Huffman-coded bitstream of dictionary lookups.
enables: Near-optimal use of the 28 param bits
Could fit 10+ common tokens in one pixel
children.risk: Need to store or agree on the Huffman table. Slight decode complexity.

children.title: Prefix code with escape
children.description: First bit = 0 means 3-bit index into top-4 common entries. First bit = 1 means next 6 bits = index into 64-entry dictionary. Hybrid: common stuff is cheap, rare stuff is possible.
enables: Common tokens cost 4 bits, rare ones cost 7 bits
Simple to decode, no table needed
children.risk: Still not as efficient as full Huffman. Fixed breakpoint may not match actual distribution.

children.title: Arithmetic Coding Strategy
children.description: Replace one strategy slot with arithmetic coding: the 28 param bits encode a fractional number that, decoded against a known symbol probability table, produces a variable-length sequence of bytes. Much denser than dictionary lookup.
enables: 28 bits can represent any sequence up to ~28 bits of entropy
Near Shannon limit for the given symbol distribution
Could encode 10-20 bytes per pixel for structured code
children.risk: Need a pre-agreed probability model. Slower decode. More complex implementation.

children.title: Static model from corpus
children.description: Build a byte-frequency model from the training corpus. 28 bits encode a range that decodes to the most likely byte sequence for that range.
enables: Deterministic, no metadata needed in pixel
Good for programs in the same language as training corpus
children.risk: Static model is suboptimal for programs that deviate from corpus distribution.

children.title: Model-switching per strategy
children.description: Strategy 0xD = Python model, 0xE = C model, 0xF = shell model. Each strategy uses a different arithmetic coding probability table optimized for that language.
enables: Language-specific compression without metadata
Common Python tokens (def, import, class) get optimal encoding in 0xD, C tokens (int, void, return) in 0xE
children.risk: Uses 3 of 16 strategy slots. Limited to 3 languages unless we expand strategy bits.

children.title: Expand Nibble Table to Full Byte Map
children.description: Current NIBBLE strategy (0x7) maps 7 nibbles to 7 bytes via a 16-entry common-symbol table. Expand: use ALL 28 param bits as 7 nibbles each selecting from a 256-entry table (using 8-bit indices packed differently). Or better: use the 28 bits to encode offsets into a larger frequency-sorted byte-pair table.
enables: 7 bytes per pixel using any byte, not just 16 common ones
Byte-pair table means each nibble emits 2 bytes = 14 bytes per pixel
children.risk: Nibble math constrains flexibility. 4 bits cant index 256 entries without multiplexing.

children.title: Byte-pair nibble strategy
children.description: 16-entry table of the most common 2-byte pairs in code. Each nibble selects a pair. 7 nibbles = 14 bytes. Ratio: 14*8/32 = 3.5:1 bits, much better than current.
enables: Double the output per pixel
Common pairs like \n\t, ()\n, ": ", "= " become single nibbles
children.risk: Need to identify the 16 most valuable byte pairs. May not cover all needed characters.

children.title: Meta-Pixel: Strategy Selects Strategy
children.description: A meta-strategy pixel that doesnt produce output directly but modifies how subsequent pixels are decoded. Example: pixel with strategy META sets the dictionary to Python-mode, and all following pixels use Python-optimized tables until another META pixel switches.
enables: One pixel to configure the entire decoding context
Language-specific modes without wasting strategy slots
Future-proof: new modes dont need new strategies, just new META payloads
children.risk: Breaks the purity of each pixel being independent. Adds state machine to decoder.

children.title: Mode-switch + dictionary swap
children.description: META pixel payload selects one of N pre-built dictionaries. Following pixels use that dictionary. Switch dictionaries mid-program for polyglot code.
enables: Optimal dictionary for each section of code
Python preamble + C inline in same image
children.risk: Must track current mode during decode. More state = more test surface.

children.title: What concrete encoding strategy improvements would help pixelpack achieve better
children.description: What concrete encoding strategy improvements would help pixelpack achieve better than 7:1 bits-to-output ratio? Current bottleneck: each 32-bit pixel only expands to ~4-5 bytes average via dictionary lookup. Ideas must work within the existing seed format (4-bit strategy selector + 28-bit params). Think about: variable-length dictionary indices, back-references to previous pixel output, arithmetic coding of common byte patterns, context-dependent expansion where pixel N's output depends on pixel N-1's output.

children.title: Stateful Markov Chained Expansion
children.description: Each pixel's strategy receives the last few bytes of the previous pixel's output as context. Strategy 0xF becomes CHAIN: its 28 bits encode an offset table mapping (previous_output_byte → dictionary_entry), so if pixel N-1 ended with common bytes like 0xB8 or 0xE8, pixel N can predict and emit the statistically likely continuation in fewer bits.
enables: Streaming programs where later fragments build on earlier ones—enables encoding real call chains (call label1; call label2) where the second call's encoding is nearly free because context predicts it
Adaptive compression that gets better on longer multi-pixel images, since more accumulated state means better prediction—enables 50:1 or higher ratios on 4x4+ images
Error-resilient decoding: the context window creates natural checkpoint boundaries where decoding can resynchronize after corruption
children.risk: Breaks the clean independence guarantee—pixel N cannot be decoded without pixel N-1's output. Makes random-access into the middle of an image impossible. Parallel decoding of pixels becomes sequential.

children.title: L-System Grammar from 28 Bits
children.description: Replace dictionary lookup with generative grammar. The 28 parameter bits define a tiny L-system: 4 bits for alphabet size (2-4 symbols), 12 bits for production rules (which symbol expands to which short byte sequence from a fixed table), 12 bits for axiom and iteration count. The decoder applies rules iteratively to grow output. A 2-symbol, 2-rule system with 3 iterations can produce highly structured, self-similar byte sequences from almost nothing.
enables: Encoding of programs with repetitive nested structure (nested loops, repeated patterns, fractal-like bytecode) that dictionary approaches cannot compress because the repetition is at multiple scales

A new aesthetic category of 'generative programs'—programs whose structure comes from grammatical expansion rather than human design—enables exploration of what programmatically-interesting code looks like
Composability: two L-system pixels can be designed so one's output feeds as the axiom for the next, creating compound grammars of arbitrary depth
children.risk: L-system output is hard to steer toward arbitrary target bytes—encoding a specific program becomes a search problem over grammar space, which may be much sparser than dictionary space. Most grammars produce nonsense.

children.title: Meta-Pixel Selects Sub-Dictionary From Pool
children.description: Dedicate strategy 0xE or the first pixel in multi-pixel images as META: its 28 bits select a specialized 32-entry sub-dictionary from a pre-built pool of ~262 million possible sub-dictionaries (28 bits of selection). The pool is built offline by clustering byte n-grams from a large program corpus. All subsequent DICT_N pixels in the same image reference this sub-dictionary instead of the global one.
enables: Domain-specific compression: one meta-pixel selects a sub-dictionary tuned for Brainfuck, another for Z80, another for x86 bootloaders—each domain gets custom-fitted entries without bloating the global dictionary
Community-contributed dictionaries: anyone can build and publish a sub-dictionary for their target platform, distributed as a simple JSON file alongside the image
Graceful migration: old images still work with the frozen global dictionary, new images opt into a specialized one—no backward compatibility break
children.risk: Goes against the 'one pixel is self-contained' philosophy. Requires a large pre-built pool that must be shipped with the decoder. Sub-dictionary selection becomes a hard optimization problem during encoding. Adds a coordination dependency between pixels.

children.title: Arithmetic Coded Variable-Width Dictionary Indices
children.description: Within a single pixel's 28 parameter bits, use 6 bits to select a frequency profile (from 64 pre-computed profiles covering different program distributions), then arithmetically decode the remaining 22 bits against that profile. Common entries get fractional-bit codes (e.g., entry 0 costs 1.2 bits, entry 31 costs 6.8 bits), so a pixel whose target uses mostly common entries can fit 8-12 dictionary references instead of the current 5-7.
enables: Near-optimal bit utilization per pixel—theoretical limit goes from fixed 5 bits/entry to the entropy of the actual distribution, typically 2.5-3.5 bits for real code corpora
Automatic adaptation: the 64 frequency profiles act as implicit domain detection—the encoder just picks whichever profile gives the best fit, no manual tuning
Foundation for entropy-aware segmentation: the segmenter can preferentially split programs at boundaries that align well with one of the 64 profiles, finding globally optimal segment points
children.risk: Arithmetic decoding is slower and more complex than table lookup—may not fit the minimalist aesthetic of the project. Floating-point or large-integer arithmetic in the decoder breaks the 'simple expansion' property. Edge cases around exact bit boundaries are notoriously buggy.

children.title: Corpus-clustered profiles with escape symbols
children.description: K-means-cluster the real program corpus into 64 groups by byte/n-gram frequency, each yielding one profile. Include an explicit escape symbol in every profile so rare bytes can be spelled out literally without wasting the common-case efficiency.
enables: Profiles are automatically grounded in actual program distributions rather than hand-tuned guesses, and can be retrained as the corpus evolves without changing the decoder spec
The escape symbol means every possible target program is encodable — no coverage holes — so this strategy can be the universal fallback for pixels that fail other strategies
Enables a feedback loop: as more programs are encoded, the profiles drift toward the real distribution, making future encodings denser over time
children.risk: 64 stored frequency tables must ship with the decoder, adding ~2-4KB to the boot machinery. Profiles are opaque — hard to reason about why a particular pixel maps to a particular program.
children.prune_reason: Duplicate of: Arithmetic Coded Variable-Width Dictionary Indices

children.title: Parameterized formula profiles, no stored tables
children.description: Instead of 64 arbitrary lookup tables, define 8 parametric distribution shapes (uniform, geometric decay, Zipf, bimodal, etc.) and use 3 bits for shape + 3 bits for a shape-specific parameter (e.g., decay rate, mode position). The decoder computes the profile from the formula at decode time.
enables: Zero storage overhead — the decoder is pure math, no tables to ship, version, or synchronize between encoder and decoder
The smooth parameter space means similar 6-bit selectors produce similar profiles, so small seed mutations produce small program mutations — useful for genetic-search seed finding
Opens the door to user-supplied profile formulas: a community could propose new shapes without requiring a format-breaking dictionary update
children.risk: Mathematical distributions may model real program byte frequencies worse than empirical tables, wasting bits. Tuning 8 formulas to cover the space well is a design problem with no automatic solution.

children.title: Profile selects sub-dictionary, arith-code within it
children.description: The 6-bit profile selector doesn't encode a full frequency distribution — it selects a subset of ~8 dictionary entries from the full set. The 22 bits then arithmetically decode a sequence constrained to only those 8 symbols. Tiny alphabet = very few bits per reference, so a pixel can pack 10-15 references.
enables: Programs with small vocabularies (which is many real programs) compress extremely well — potentially doubling or tripling the bytes-per-pixel ratio compared to fixed-width dictionary indices
The sub-dictionary selection IS the semantic fingerprint of the program — enables fast similarity search by comparing which 8-entry subsets two pixels reference

A second decoding pass could check multiple overlapping sub-dictionaries for the same bitstream, enabling one pixel to decode to multiple valid programs (intentional polysemy for creative/generative use)
children.risk: Programs that need many distinct dictionary entries are impossible to encode with a single 8-symbol sub-dictionary. The subset-selection encoding (choosing 8 from 96) itself costs log2(C(96,8)) ≈ 33 bits — more than the 6 bits available, so some lossy approximation is needed.
children.prune_reason: Duplicate of: Arithmetic Coded Variable-Width Dictionary Indices

children.title: Arithmetic meta-strategy emitting sub-strategy seeds
children.description: The 22 arith-decoded bits don't produce dictionary entries directly — they produce a sequence of (strategy_id, compressed_params) pairs that each invoke one of the existing 16 expansion strategies. The frequency profile determines which strategies are cheap. This strategy is a meta-strategy that composes other strategies within a single pixel.
enables: Turns the 16 existing strategies into composable primitives — a single pixel can mix DICT_N, NIBBLE, and RLE segments, picking whichever is cheapest for each piece of the target
New strategies can be adopted without changing the meta-strategy — they just become another cheap/expensive symbol in the profile
Enables strategy-chaining patterns like 'use DICT_3 for the header, then RLE for the body' encoded fractally in one 32-bit seed
children.risk: Adds a recursive decoding layer — each pixel's expansion now involves arithmetic decoding THEN sub-strategy expansion. Complexity and debuggability suffer. The overhead of encoding strategy IDs may eat the gains from fractional-bit coding.

children.title: Cross-pixel profile inheritance via delta encoding
children.description: In multi-pixel images, pixel 0's 6-bit profile is absolute. Each subsequent pixel's 6-bit selector is a delta — it specifies how to mutate the previous pixel's profile (e.g., 'swap the two most-frequent symbols', 'rotate frequencies by 3 positions', 'merge with profile 0x1F'). The 22 data bits are decoded against the inherited profile.
enables: Multi-pixel programs share statistical structure — the inheritance captures this for free, giving better compression per pixel than N independent arith-code passes

Enables a family-resemblance aesthetic: pixels in the same image produce programs that are byte-distribution-similar, which could be exploited for visual/thematic coherence in generative art applications

The delta vocabulary (how profiles mutate) is a new design surface that can be tuned independently of the base profiles — could encode domain-specific transformations like 'switch from data section to code section frequencies'
children.risk: Decoding is no longer embarrassingly parallel across pixels — pixel N depends on pixel N-1's decoded profile. Breaks the current independent-expansion model. Error in one pixel cascades to all subsequent pixels.

children.title: Inter-Pixel XOR as Information Channel
children.description: The decoder reads pairs of adjacent pixels and XORs their 28-bit parameter fields to extract a second, independent 28-bit value. Pixel A's params encode one set of dictionary entries; (A XOR B)'s params encode a completely different set. Two pixels that look visually different can carry up to 56 bits of independent information through their 28-bit param fields plus their relationship.
enables: Doubles effective parameter space without changing the image format or seed structure—every 2-pixel pair carries 56 bits instead of 28, enabling longer or more complex programs from small images
Steganographic mode: the pixels look like a normal image (the XOR relationship is invisible), enabling programs hidden in plain sight within any image—enables artistic and security applications
Error detection for free: if both the direct and XOR channels are used redundantly, any single-pixel corruption can be detected and corrected by checking consistency between the two channels
children.risk: Tight coupling between pixels—if either pixel in a pair is modified, both channels are destroyed. Encoding becomes a constraint-satisfaction problem (find pair of pixels where both direct values and their XOR are meaningful). Visual coherence of the image becomes harder to control.

children.title: Chain XOR with cascading decode
children.description: Pixels are decoded in sequence: pixel 0 is direct, pixel 1 XOR pixel 0 yields a second payload, pixel 2 XOR pixel 1 yields a third, etc. N pixels produce 2N-1 independent 28-bit payloads (N direct + N-1 XOR-derived) through a single linear pass.
enables: A 4x4 image jumps from 16 payloads to 31 payloads, pushing max program size toward ~400 bytes without changing the image dimensions or strategy format.

Segmenter can assign short segments to direct pixels and long/tail segments to XOR-derived payloads, creating a natural priority hierarchy where direct encodings are primary and XOR encodings are overflow.
Decoding remains O(N) and stateless per pixel — the cascade is trivially parallelizable since each XOR only depends on its immediate neighbor.
children.risk: A single corrupted pixel breaks the chain from that point forward; the decode is fragile in a way direct-only isn't. Also constrains pixel ordering — can't freely rearrange pixels without breaking XOR meaning.
children.prune_reason: Duplicate of: Inter-Pixel XOR as Information Channel

children.title: Constraint-satisfaction pixel solver
children.description: Instead of greedily assigning seeds then computing XOR as bonus, define ALL target payloads upfront (both direct and pairwise XOR) and solve for RGBA values that satisfy every constraint simultaneously. This is a SAT/constraint problem over the pixel grid.
enables: Full pairwise XOR: for N pixels, potentially N direct + N*(N-1)/2 XOR-derived channels — a 4x4 image could theoretically carry 136 independent 28-bit payloads, enabling programs of several KB from a tiny image.
The solver can discover pixel arrangements where even non-adjacent XOR relationships are meaningful, turning the image into a dense information lattice rather than a linear chain.
Opens the door to error-correcting constraints: add redundancy where multiple XOR paths decode to the same payload, making the encoding resilient to pixel corruption.
children.risk: SAT solving is computationally expensive — encode time could blow up. May not always find solutions for arbitrary payload combinations. The complexity could make the encoder fragile and hard to debug.

children.title: Traversal-keyed multi-program images
children.description: The image stores XOR relationships along multiple paths. A small 'traversal key' (which pixel pairs to XOR, in what order) selects which program the image decodes to. The same PNG file is multiple programs depending on which key you provide.
enables: Polyglot images: a single 2x2 PNG could be a Python script, a shell command, AND a Brainfuck program, selected by traversal key — the image is a programmable switchboard.
Steganographic layers: the 'obvious' decode path produces a benign program, while a hidden traversal key reveals the actual payload — plausible deniability for encoded content.
Program variants encoded as different traversal keys over the same pixel data — versioning, A/B testing, or conditional execution without storing multiple images.
children.risk: Key distribution becomes a separate problem — the key must be communicated out-of-band. Validation becomes much harder (must verify every possible traversal). Debugging is nightmarish when the same bytes mean different things under different traversals.

children.title: Self-deriving XOR with accumulated state
children.description: Each pixel's XOR partner isn't the next pixel but is determined by the accumulated decoded bytes so far. The first few pixels decode directly and produce output bytes; those bytes select which earlier pixel to XOR with the current pixel, making the decode path self-determining.
enables: The image literally contains its own decode instructions — earlier output selects later decode strategy, creating a bootstrapping loop similar to how the pixel strategies already work but at the meta-level of which XOR relationships to use.
Variable-length decoding: the image can early-terminate or extend its own decode based on what it's already produced, enabling programs that are exactly the right length without padding.
Fractal information density: as decoded bytes feed back into XOR partner selection, the effective information per pixel grows with program length — longer programs get more efficient encoding.
children.risk: Decoding is no longer parallelizable — strict sequential dependency. A single wrong byte cascades into wrong partner selection, corrupting everything downstream. The encoder must simulate the entire decode to find valid pixel assignments.

children.title: XOR as visual secret-sharing
children.description: Split program encoding across two separate PNG images. Image A encodes a program directly; Image B is a different program directly; but the pixel-by-pixel XOR of corresponding positions in A and B reveals a THIRD independent program. Each share is independently valid.
enables: Distributed program storage: two parties each hold one image, neither can extract the hidden program alone, but combining them (XOR) reveals it — cryptographic secret-sharing without traditional cryptography.
A library of 'share images' that can be paired with any encoded image to reveal different hidden programs — the share image acts as a decryption key that is itself a valid pixelpack image.
Collaborative creation: two artists each create a pixelpack image, the combined XOR produces an emergent third work that neither anticipated — a generative art collaboration primitive.
children.risk: Requires both images to have identical dimensions and alignment. The hidden program's existence is detectable through statistical analysis of either share. Increases the conceptual complexity of the format significantly.
